title: Homestead End Table
summary: A small end table with angled 2x4 legs, a thick 2x8 top, and an open bottom shelf — sturdy enough to double as a stool.
description:
This is the smallest piece in the Homestead Collection, alongside the Homestead Bench and Homestead Console Table already in this catalog — same 10° splayed-leg language, scaled down to end-table size, and built solidly enough that it doubles as a step stool or extra seating in a pinch.

The **top cleats and legs share the same 10° angle** as the rest of the collection, so a test cut and dry-fit before committing to the full set is worth the five minutes it takes — a 2x4 is cheap, a redone leg set after the top is already attached is not.

Pocket holes keep every fastener hidden inside the joint; if you don't have a pocket-hole jig, self-tapping structural screws driven from the outside work as a fallback, just less invisibly.

tools.note: Or a miter saw that bevels — every angled piece shares the same 10° cut.

cutList.note: Both ends 10° off square, long point to long point, ends NOT parallel.
Both ends 10° off square, long point to short point, ends parallel.
Both ends 10° off square, long point to long point, ends NOT parallel.
Both ends 10° off square, long point to long point, ends NOT parallel.

steps.title: Cut all pieces
steps.body: Cut two 21" tabletop boards from the 2x8 and two 19" shelf boards from the 2x6, both square. Everything else is 2x4 and every angled cut is the same 10deg, so set the saw once: two 13-1/2" cleats and two 12-1/4" mid pieces and two 7-5/8" short pieces all measure long point to long point with their ends NOT parallel, while the four 17-1/4" long legs measure long point to short point with their ends parallel — that difference is the whole trick, so mark each blank before you cut it. Count your stock first: those 2x4 parts add up to 135-3/4", so one 8 ft board will not cover them and you need two. Cut one leg, one mid and one short piece, dry-fit them, and confirm the assembly stands the way you want before cutting the other seven — a 2x4 is cheap and a redone leg set after the top is on is not.

steps.title: Attach the cleats to the tabletop
steps.body: Lay the two 21" tabletop boards side by side face down; at 7-1/4" each they make a top 14-1/2" wide. Lay the two 13-1/2" cleats across the underside, running the short way — 13-1/2" of cleat on a 14-1/2" top leaves 1/2" of top proud at each end, so set them in that far on both sides rather than eyeballing it. Orient both cleats so their 10deg ends lean the same way, since that angle is what the leg assemblies register against. Fasten with 2-3/4" self-tapping structural screws and glue, two per board per cleat, driving from the cleat into the tabletop so nothing shows on the finished top.

steps.title: Build the leg assemblies
steps.body: Build two leg assemblies, each from two 17-1/4" long legs, one 12-1/4" mid piece and one 7-5/8" short piece. The two cross pieces are different lengths because the 10deg splay changes the spacing between the legs at different heights, so dry-clamp one assembly and find where each piece actually lands before you drill anything — the plan does not state which sits high and which sits low, and the dry-fit is what settles it. Drill 1-1/2" pocket holes in the ends of the cross pieces and drive 2-1/2" pocket screws into the legs so every fastener is hidden inside the joint; if you have no jig, 2-3/4" structural screws from the outside face work, just visibly. Build the second assembly against the first as a template so the pair matches.

steps.title: Attach the legs to the top
steps.body: Stand both leg assemblies against the underside of the tabletop cleats, one at each cleat, with the 10deg faces meeting flush — the cleat angle and the leg angle are the same cut, so a gap here means one of the two is off and it is worth fixing now. Fasten with 2-1/2" pocket screws through the assembly into the cleats. With 17-1/4" legs under a 1-1/2" thick top the table finishes 18-3/4" tall. Turn it upright on a flat floor and check that it does not rock before adding the shelf.

steps.title: Add the bottom shelf
steps.body: Set the two 19" 2x6 shelf boards between the leg assemblies, side by side to make a shelf 11" wide, resting on the lower cross pieces. Clamp them in place and check the table for square and for rock before fastening — the shelf is what stops the splayed legs from spreading, so whatever position it locks in is the position the table keeps. Fasten from underneath with 2-3/4" structural screws and glue so no screw head shows on the shelf surface.

steps.title: Sand and finish
steps.body: Sand every surface and ease all the edges and corners, particularly the tabletop's ends and the outside faces of the legs, since this piece is built to double as a stool and gets handled. Vacuum and wipe the dust off before finishing or the stain will carry it. Apply the stain to match the rest of the Homestead Collection, finishing the underside and the shelf's bottom face as well as the visible surfaces so the boards take on moisture evenly.
